Why did 70% of merchants drop?

A research-led investigation into the adoption failure of KiotViet's Food App feature — and how contextual inquiry uncovered a product strategy problem hiding behind a usability problem.

Timeline Sep 2025
Method Contextual Inquiry
Sample 08 small-to-medium F&B merchants using delivery platforms
Outcome Feature Revamp
30%
Adoption Funnel
100% — API Connected
All merchants who attempted setup
70% — Menu Synced
30% dropped at this step
30% — Active
Only 3 in 10 received orders
00 — What I Brought to the Table

04 key contributions.

Problem Reframing

Reframed the problem and aligned stakeholders around the right research question

Research Strategy

Defined and led the research strategy and methodology

Behavioral Segmentation

Conducted behavioral segmentation to map merchant workflows

Decision-Enabled Insights

Delivered insights that revealed issues across UI, information architecture, and product strategy, leading to major feature revamps

01 — The Problem

Six months post-launch,
adoption had stalled.

Launched in March 2025, Food App was built to automate synchronisation between POS systems and delivery platforms — eliminating manual order entry. The feature was highly anticipated and was expected to drive merchant retention for KiotViet's F&B segment.

KiotViet Food App mechanism
Hover each step to see the meaning
Customer places an order via GrabFood, ShopeeFood, etc.
Order Placed on delivery apps
Order data auto-syncs to KiotViet POS system
Synced to POS sales & inventory data
Kitchen receives and prepares the order
Kitchen Processing the order
Order packaged and handed off for delivery
Delivery to customer

Six months after launch, the numbers told a harsh story.

Active adoption rate
30%

Of merchants who connected via API actually received orders. The vast majority never completed setup despite full connectivity.

Support volume
Surging

CS tickets related to Food App rose rapidly post-launch. Sales teams spent significant time troubleshooting instead of acquiring new merchants.

Average setup time
18 min

Per interviewed merchant before redesign. One operator with 60+ franchises spent nearly a week on manual syncing — and still encountered errors throughout.

02 — Reframing the Research

The right method
for the right user.

The PM's initial request was to survey the sales team — quick, low-cost. But this revealed a flawed organisational assumption: that understanding salespeople's frustrations was equivalent to understanding merchants' problems.

✕ Initial request

"Survey the Sales Team"

Salespeople are constantly on the road — low response quality, compressed nuance. They experience merchant pain secondhand, filtered through their own incentives. Surveys reduce complex workflows into checkbox answers. Fast, but answers the wrong question for the wrong user group.

✓ Reframed approach

"Go Watch Merchants Work"

Contextual inquiry inside real stores. Observe the exact moment where 70% of merchants dropped off — after API connection. First-hand operational context no survey could surface. The right question: what actually happens during setup?

"Who are we serving — merchants, or salesmen?"

— Key reframing question posed to the product team

03 — Behavioral Segmentation

Four merchant types.
One product assumption.

Field research revealed merchants were not a monolith. Mapping real workflows surfaced four distinct behavioral segments — and a critical mismatch. The feature assumed all merchants wanted data synchronisation; most were optimising for order fulfillment speed.

Group 01

Menu Separators

Manage online and offline menus as completely separate systems. Aggregate sales data manually at day or month end.

"Our store just needs delivery orders to appear on POS for label printing."

Highest Friction ⚠
Group 02

Menu Duplicators

Create duplicate items in POS — one for in-store, one for delivery — with different SKUs, causing messy inventory and sales reconciliation.

"My accountant still manually reconciles stock and revenue at day's end."

Data Integrity Issues
Group 03

POS-Reliant Operators

Depend on POS for kitchen ticket printing. Without sync, cashiers manually re-enter each delivery order — error-prone on busy days.

"On busy days, staff enter wrong items or miss discounts — sales data goes wrong."

Manual Re-entry Risk
Group 04

POS Bypass Operators

Skip POS entirely. Carry their phone into the kitchen and write labels by hand. The app overwhelms them visually when orders pile up.

"We don't have time to enter orders — we just take the phone to the kitchen."

Order Fulfillment Risk

Tap each card to highlight

04 — Workflow Mapping

Four merchant types.
Two core workflows.

Despite the differences across segments, merchant operations eventually converged into two core workflows. The Food App feature was designed around the second workflow, assuming merchants would synchronize menus and process orders through POS. However, many operated closer to the first workflow, bypassing POS entirely.

Workflow 1
⏭ POS Bypass
1

Receive order on merchant app

2

Prepare order

3

Label order with hand-written ticket

4

Ship

Workflow 2
🔗 POS Integrated
1

Receive order on merchant app

2

Input order on POS

3

Print kitchen & order tickets from POS

4

Prepare order

5

Label order with printed ticket

6

Ship

05 — The Core Failure

Three mental models.
Zero alignment.

To complete setup, merchants had to understand and reconcile three incompatible conceptual architectures simultaneously. The feature implicitly asked them to think like engineers — a textbook case of mental model mismatch at product strategy level.

🧑‍🍳 How merchants think
1

Source ingredients

2

Prepare finished dish

3

Sell to customer

Simple, product-centric, operations-first

💻 KiotViet F&B POS model
1

Create base product (Hàng Thường)

2

Create product attributes (Thuộc Tính — variants, sizes, toppings)

3

Configure unit, price list & inventory

4

Sell to customer

Complex, data-centric — engineering logic required

📱 Delivery app model
1

Create main dish (Món)

2

Create topping groups (Nhóm Topping)

3

Sell to customer

Simpler — but structurally incompatible with KiotViet's SKU system

06 — Layered Findings

The problem existed
at every level.

Research revealed that low adoption was not caused by a single issue. It was the compounded result of failures across three distinct layers — each reinforcing the others.

Surface

Ambiguous UI labels, icons & error messages

Many interface elements were confusing in context. For example, when merchants synced items with intentionally different prices — standard practice to absorb delivery commission fees — the system displayed: "Lệch giá bán. Hãy kiểm tra lại món đã liên kết" ("There is an item with a price mismatch"). Most merchants ignored it. Others incorrectly adjusted prices to remove the warning, causing real pricing errors across platforms. The system flagged an expected, intentional condition as an error.

Flow

Setup flow had no clear start, end, or visible progress

Merchants described feeling "trapped in a maze." Every item had to be matched manually — down to toppings, sizes, ice levels, and sweetness. A 60-franchise operator spent nearly a week on this and still encountered errors. Another spent an afternoon afraid to leave the screen, worried progress would be lost. No visible progress. No clear end state. No recovery path.

Architecture

Product data structure incompatible with merchant mental model

The feature inherited KiotViet's F&B data model — built around SKU codes, product attributes, and inventory units. Merchants think in terms of ingredients → finished dish → sale. When syncing items across platforms, they had to reconcile different naming conventions, different pricing structures, and fundamentally different menu hierarchies. This is system engineering logic, not food business logic. No amount of UI polish could fix this mismatch.

Strategy

Feature built on the wrong core Jobs-to-Be-Done

The deepest failure: the product was designed around data synchronisation, but merchants primarily needed operational speed. Many segments — particularly Menu Separators and POS Bypass Operators — had no interest in synchronisation. They simply needed orders to appear on screen quickly and accurately. The feature solved a problem merchants did not have, while creating new ones they did not ask for.

07 — Product Changes & Results

Three improvements.
Dramatic outcomes.

In December 2025, four months after the study, Food App was redesigned. The changes addressed all three problem layers simultaneously.

Efficiency

Stepper Navigation

Clear entry and exit points, visible progress status, and defined expectations at each step. Merchants knew where they were — and that there was an end.

Satisfaction

AI-Assisted Item Matching

Automated detection and matching of corresponding menu items across systems. The single highest-friction step in the entire flow was eliminated.

Learnability

Familiar Mental Model

Redesigned around the "Main Dish – Toppings" structure merchants already use on delivery platforms. The product now speaks the merchant's language.

Metric
Before  (Aug 2025)
After  (Dec 2025)
Average Setup Time
18 min
2 min
First-Attempt Success Rate
0%
100%
Sample Size
8 users
7 users

Note: These are usability test results, not large-scale deployment metrics. Broader post-redesign adoption data was not included in the case study.

08 — Reflection

What this case
really teaches.

Sometimes, usability problems can signal deeper product strategy failures. This research did not just improve a flow — it diagnosed a strategic misalignment between what the product was built to do and what merchants actually needed.

Lesson 01

Survey the right users, not the most accessible ones.

Lesson 02

Behavioral segmentation reveals what static personas obscure.

Lesson 03

Architecture mismatches cannot be fixed with better copy.

Good B2B products do not ask users to change how they work. They adapt to the way users already run their business.

Food App was built around a technical capability — data synchronisation — rather than merchant behaviour. Merchants had self-invented workflows refined over years of running their operations. Any product that forces abandonment of those workflows will face resistance. The fix was not faster onboarding. It was rethinking what the product was actually for.

Khanh-Ha Luu  ·  2025  ·  Viet Food App  ·  UX Case Study
